title: Getting Started with Zone 10b Gardening in Clearwater
author: Debbie
excerpt: Everything you need to know to start your garden in Clearwater, FL. From understanding our unique climate to picking your first plants.
content: If you're new to gardening in Clearwater, Florida, welcome to one of the most rewarding growing zones in the country! USDA Zone 10b means our minimum winter temperatures stay between 35-40 degrees Fahrenheit, which opens the door to an incredible variety of tropical and subtropical plants.

The first thing to understand is that our growing calendar is flipped compared to northern states. While they're planting tomatoes in May, we plant ours in October. Our 'summer' is actually too hot for many vegetables, but it's when our tropical fruits like mangoes and papayas really shine.

Start with the basics: good sandy soil amended with compost, a reliable irrigation system (afternoon thunderstorms help but aren't enough), and mulch, mulch, mulch. The Florida sun is intense, and mulch is your best friend for retaining moisture and keeping roots cool.

For beginners, I recommend starting with these foolproof Zone 10b plants: Swiss chard (grows nearly year-round), pentas (flowers that attract butterflies), a citrus tree (nothing beats homegrown oranges), and lemongrass (practically indestructible and makes great tea).
Check out the Plant Database on this site to explore all the options, and use the Planting Journal to track your progress. Happy gardening!

comments.author: Maria
comments.text: Great intro! I just moved to Clearwater and had no idea the growing season was so different here.

comments.author: Jim
comments.text: Swiss chard is definitely the way to go for beginners. Mine has been going strong for 8 months!

title: 5 Medicinal Herbs Every Florida Garden Should Have
author: Debbie
excerpt: These five medicinal plants thrive in Zone 10b and can help with everything from sunburn to stress. Plus they're beautiful in the garden.
content: One of the things I love most about gardening in Zone 10b is that so many medicinal plants absolutely thrive here. Our warm, humid climate is perfect for tropical herbs that people in northern zones can only dream about growing outdoors.
Here are my top 5 picks for medicinal herbs in the Clearwater garden:

1. ALOE VERA - This is the plant everyone should have. Sunburn from a beach day? Snap off a leaf and apply the gel directly. It also makes a refreshing smoothie ingredient. Aloe practically takes care of itself in our sandy soil.

2. TURMERIC - Plant the rhizomes in spring and by winter you'll be harvesting bright orange roots. Golden milk made with fresh turmeric is on another level compared to store-bought powder. The plants are gorgeous too, with large tropical leaves.
3. LEMONGRASS - Doubles as a culinary herb and a natural mosquito deterrent (and we definitely need that in Florida!). The tea is incredibly calming, and the stalks are essential for Thai cooking.

4. HOLY BASIL (TULSI) - This adaptogen herb for stress relief grows like a weed here. I have it in several spots around the garden. The tea is wonderful and it's considered sacred in Ayurvedic medicine.
5. GINGER - Another rhizome that loves our climate. Fresh ginger tea with honey is my go-to for cold season, and the plants add lovely tropical foliage to shady spots.
Visit the Medicinal Plants section of the Plant Database for detailed growing info and recipes for each of these herbs.

comments.author: Sandra
comments.text: I had no idea you could grow turmeric in Florida! Ordering rhizomes now.

title: Spring 2026 Planting Guide for Clearwater
author: Debbie
excerpt: What to plant this spring in Zone 10b, what to harvest, and how to prepare your garden for the hot months ahead.
content: Spring is one of the most exciting times in a Zone 10b garden. The cool-season crops are finishing up, and it's time to transition to warm-season planting.

WHAT TO PLANT NOW (March-April): This is the sweet spot for heat-loving plants. Get your peppers, okra, and sweet potato slips in the ground. Plant tropical fruits like papaya, banana, and passion fruit. All your medicinal herbs (turmeric, ginger, lemongrass, holy basil) should go in now too.
WHAT TO HARVEST: If you planted cool-season crops in the fall, now is harvest time! Pull those carrots, cut that lettuce, and enjoy your last spinach before it bolts in the warming temperatures.
PREPARE FOR SUMMER: By May, the real heat arrives. Get your irrigation system checked, stock up on mulch, and make sure your tropical plants are well-established before the dog days hit.
One of my favorite things to do in spring is plant a new fruit tree. This year I'm adding a lychee tree to the yard. They take a few years to fruit but the wait is so worth it.
Remember to check the Planting Journal to log everything you're putting in the ground. Future you will thank present you for keeping good records!
